title: Reframe the label as censored time-to-pit and convert back to next-lap hazard
summary: Pit-stop timing is naturally a time-to-event problem.
rationale: Pit-stop timing is naturally a time-to-event problem. XGBoost supports Cox and AFT survival objectives with censoring, and F1 tyre-degradation work explicitly models pit stops as state resets. A survival target uses more structure than plain next-lap classification because every non-pit lap also contains information about how far the stop still is.
implementation_hint: For each driver-race-stint row, compute `time_to_next_pit` when an eventual pit exists; mark rows near session end without another observed pit as right-censored. Train a survival model or discrete hazard model on stint-level sequences, then extract the implied hazard for horizon 1 as `P(PitNextLap=1)`. Blend that hazard with the existing binary classifier.
expected_effect: Better ordering near the pit window, especially on long stints where the current classifier may treat many negative rows as similar.
risk: Label construction is easy to get wrong around final laps, red-flag-like anomalies, or test-time censoring assumptions; a bad conversion back to next-lap probability can erase the benefit.
